sentences.text: What did I tell you was next?
Yeah, I already mentioned Graditz.
Dachau, right.
That's where I got progressively worse.
The only thing is, let's start with Auschwitz.
I got there in the end of '44, so we-- or summer-- I mean fall of '44.
So, there's a gap from '43 to '44, and I don't quite know how to fill that gap.
They were SS guards, and they were extremely strict.
And they treated us-- by us, I should say, it was by now a mixture of people from all over Europe.
They were Polish Jews.
They were Hungarian Jews.
They were even Russian prisoners, not Jews.
They were all part of this conglomeration of inmates.
And we were all treated with great disdain, and great total lack of human dignity.
I mean, that's putting it mildly.
People were beaten.
We were marched every morning on the way to work.
And if somebody could not manage to march, you know, he would fall down or something, but he was forced to walk.
And many times, people just would die on the way out to work.
They just were not in condition to either go to work or march, or be subject to being beaten.
So, people were dying all around us from malnutrition, from mistreatment, and from illness.
I never saw one doctor in-- except for one time in any of the camps that I was in that would tend to anybody being sick.
People that were sick were left to die.
What was a typical day like in that camp?
I mean, when did you get up in the morning, and what happened?
You got up at daybreak.
And there was what they called appell, which was a call to gather in usually a large, flat courtyard or court area.
And they would call names.
And, and they would say, gather your-- order us around to go and march to, to work.
There was a certain formality in this appell, which means a call to order, which happened daily.
Well, my clothing at that time, they were mostly rags that were tied together, depending on the time of the year.
If it was wintertime, there were more rags.
And it wasn't until we got to Auschwitz that we actually got a uniform-- you know, the blue and white striped uniform.
But in these camps, pre-Auschwitz and after Auschwitz, we just wore whatever we could manage to, to put together to protect our bodies.
Did not even all have shoes.
Sometimes we had rags wrapped around our feet.
I had that at one time, but I was able to, I think, usually have some kind of shoe on me.
Maybe not matching, but shoes.
And very often not enough clothing or rags to keep the body warm, so that we had to duck for cover at times, or we, we tried to acquire more rags to cover our bodies.
Right.
I was in a number--
Well, there in Graditz I was in the beginning of '43.

And then for about a year and a half, I was in a series of camps in the Sudeten area of Czechoslovakia, which were the mountainous-- the mountainous region between Germany and Czechoslovakia, which had a lot of forced labor camps.
But I don't-- in my mind, I don't remember every single one of them, until I got to Auschwitz.
That was so-- became so much more traumatic that the other just left my memory.
Heavy labor.
Heavy labor in each of those caps.
In those camps they were all men.
I never saw any women.
Some of them were very large.
They ranged from like 2,000 down to 300 men.
They were not the large camps of Auschwitz and Dachau.
These were all smaller camps.
If not worse.
If not worse.
OK, then what happened, what sticks in my mind was really my arrival in Auschwitz.
And I can't even tell you where I came from, but it was in late-- it was in the fall of 1944, maybe September or maybe late August.
And I arrived at a station platform, and masses of people would exit to the platform.
And there was three different Germans in dress uniform with the boots and canes and so on.

One of them, it turned out afterwards, was Mengele, along with two or three other officers who were directing as the masses of people would come from the trains, people going to one side, and people going to the other side.
I had no knowledge at the time-- none of us did-- as to why or what would happen to either of the categories of people, except we all know now what was happening.
And I had also no knowledge of the crematoria that were there, right there, or of gassing, or of Zyklon, or any of that kind of stuff.
It was just not told to us, and we weren't aware of it.
But I ended up being on the side that ended up living.
And I was healthy, so to speak.
I was-- I was not sick.
I was undernourished, but I was able to walk.
I was independent.
And I had-- I had it all together up in my brain.

A lot of people that perished-- even then and before then-- perished because they were not able to think straight and protect either their hygiene, or use common sense to, to not be caught, you know, and beaten up.
I was able to do that.
For some reason, I possessed a certain survivorship, not all of which is something that you have.
Some of survival happens because of luck and because of circumstances that you have no control over.

But beyond that, the instances where I had to make decisions that ended up saving my life proved that, for some reason, I had a certain mental state that, that made it possible for me to exercise judgment that benefited me.
Nothing-- that happened almost every day.
Almost every day.
When I saw people next to me who would, you know, like defecate in their pants, or not conduct proper hygiene, I knew that that was one way to become ill.
That was one way to lose your own self-respect.

I can't really describe specifically what I did, except that I do remember that in my-- basically in my common sense that I was able to generate, I knew that this whole situation wasn't going to last forever.
So I had confidence that this terrible affront to humanity would end.
And it gave me enough confidence to keep generating the common sense that I felt and knew I had to muster to exercise judgment.
As best as I could.
There was some water.
You had to go and look for it sometimes.
And depending on the camp-- as a matter of fact, in Auschwitz, which I talked about, that part was, was relatively civilized.
They had toilets.
And they had all that kind of stuff, which was not available in all camps.
In many camps you just went behind a tree, or you went in a hole, or you whatever, depending on the time of the year, too.
It was very regimented.
And you had to do that at certain times.
You could sometimes not even excuse yourself during work time.
You had to organize when to prepare for that, and when not to prepare for that.
So, it was totally regimented.
But when you are hungry, and when you are physically deprived-- never mind dignity-wise deprived-- you lose all common sense of what is doable and what is not doable.
And that's really what does you in.
I was able to-- along with a few other people, I was able to maintain a minimum of common sense that kept me above the level of animal.
Basically, human beings, when they lose that, they become animals.
Arrived at Auschwitz.
And did they give you a number at that point?
They gave me a number, which is right here on my arm.
Do you remember that process?
Yes, I do.
The man had a stencil-like tool, very much like what you do when you get tattooed.
I had seen it when I was younger.
It's a form of tattooing where you inject dye or an ink into the-- into the skin with a needle.
And he kept pushing it.
It was a manual tool.
It was not an electric one.
No, no.
This was a German.
So Germany-- he was not even a soldier.
He was a civil person, I mean, a non-military person.
Not really.
I don't know if tattooing is painful.
It was-- it was tolerable.
By that time, you know, as far as offense to the senses, you know, I was pretty numb.
And while I felt bad, the fact that I was alive, that I was given food-- by that time we were, by the way, moved from Auschwitz to Birkenau, which was a few miles away.
In Auschwitz, I was only there maybe three or four days.
Birkenau is where I ended up in a barracks.
It was pretty sizable.
I was told even then, that at one time these barracks were used by horses.
They had these large stables that were used by the Poles.
Birkenau and Auschwitz actually is today in Poland.
And prior to the Second World War, it was Polish as well.
And the Poles are known to be good with horses.
Anyway, we knew things were going on there that were different from other camps.
First of all, it was very large.
It was huge.
There must have been-- oh, just guessing, I couldn't see all the people-- but there were barracks, upon barracks, upon barracks.
It was a field of large barracks, where thousands of people were.
And it wasn't until after I left Auschwitz and Birkenau, that I realized personally what else was happening there in connection with the burning and the gassing.
I didn't know that at the time I was there.
All I know-- all I knew at the time is there were piles of clothing near where we were that were organized by some of the camp inmates as to what valuables they may have contained.
And I don't know whose clothing they were.
It turned out later that they were clothing from people who were asked to go take a shower.
And then they were really Zyklon gassed to death, and then burned.
I didn't know that until afterwards.
And I also didn't know why I, with a few others, was spared, and I was in that particular series of barracks, and I didn't even go to work.
I was there from to late August of '44 until December '44-- about four months, three and a half months.
It was like there was a moment of almost rest.
It was frightful, now in hindsight, to realize where this rest took place.
And I remember Hungarian Jews arriving in large masses, large transports of Hungarian Jews.
In 1944, late 1944, is when Hungary and that part of Europe, and Island of Rhodes-- it was almost before, just before the end of the war.
They were still-- they were still gathering Jews there.
I saw them arrive, the families and all, and all very well.
They were not undernourished.
They were people that had just come from their normal way of life-- normal, quote, unquote, but relatively normal way of life.
And they were-- you know, had all their hair, and they were some of them overweight and so on.
So, they had just come from that kind of life.
And the Hungarian Jews were annihilated pretty quickly.
They would basically take them directly to the--
Right.
Right.
Well, I know that there was no organized activity.
And I ran into my friend, the one that I had not seen in over a year, who lived with a kapo.
Kapo stands for kamp polizei, K-A-P-O, two syllables, and turned into a homosexual.
In the camp.
And what made him survive was that he was allowing for the camp commandant, you know, to be his lover.
My friend, who was a perfectly normal person up until, I guess, then.
And while I had mentioned his name, I really should keep that as a private thing.
But as he was freed-- he did survive the war.
He also went to another camp after Auschwitz.
He did survive the war, but he could not come to grips with all of what happened to him.
And while he married and had a family and had three children, I see him about every other year- lives in Israel-- and he is on medication.
They were minimal.
They were minimal.
Not as bad as in some of the camps or in what I would see after that one.
They were straw.
They were burlap bags with straw in them with people being on-- in this particular series of barracks-- three rows with wooden posts in between.
And there were no organized activities during the time I was there.
Yes, there were appells, oh, yes.
Same routine.
Same routine.
Trying to think what other organized activity we had there.
I don't remember what else happened in Birkenau.
The food was a bit better than it had been up to now.
That's why I called it in the beginning of this comment, it was like a little rest period.
It was also pretty well known, in hindsight again, that by the end of '44, the Russians would free Auschwitz.
The Russians walked into Auschwitz, and I believe it was December of '44.
I had left then.
I had left before the Russians came.
I did not know.
But they asked at the time, volunteers-- it was in December of '44-- volunteers of going to a new camp in Bavaria, and leave the scene of Birkenau.
It would be better for all of us, something to that effect.
And I did volunteer.
And that's when I came to Dachau.
I didn't know what to believe.
I did learn, over the years that I was in camp, that a change of camp or a change of activity somehow kept me going.
There was something in this constant change of scenery and adaptation to new conditions ended up being good.
And I looked for that.
And so, I grabbed the chance to volunteer and go to Dachau with the group of-- how big was the transport?
I don't know.
It wasn't that large.
It was 300, 400 people on a train.
How did you-- so you got there by train?
To Dachau.
Well, it was a pretty long ride.
It was two nights.
It was a long train ride.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the first camp, it was actually Kaufering.
K-A-U-F-E-R-I-N-G.
In that first camp, conditions were pretty awful.
You could see that people were being mistreated.
People were being forced to work in substandard conditions, with less than normal food.
And Kaufering, by the way, has, oh, maybe eight, ten different camps.
It was a large sub-camp of Dachau.
And this was just one of them.
Typical day was at daybreak, there was what they called appell, which means that you had to rally and stand outside, regardless of the weather, and be-- wait for your name to be called.
And, and then you soon after, would rally to go to work.
They would always first have a call of, of rally to mention the names.
And then the kommandos would go out to work at the various groups.
Yeah, kommandos, so that's what the Germans called it.
They were contingents of work groups.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Later on, in the last camp in and around Dachau is when my function, just before we were liberated, when I was the leader of what was called a Totenkommando.
Totenkommando was a contingent of six or eight people that buried corpses daily.

By this time, conditions were so bad and so awful everywhere, and people were dying not because they were gassed or that they were beaten, but because they were sick, and because no one took care of them.
They just-- they just died because there was no medical attention.
Either they were sick from dysentery, dysentery, or they were sick for other reasons.
It's hard to say.
I can't.
God must have been watching over me.
Yes.
I felt-- I had a strong faith.
And I felt, as I mentioned before, that this inhumane-- to put it mildly-- set of conditions could not last.
It was doomed to end.
I was only praying that I could outlast that period until the ending took place, which fortunately I did.
I was never really beaten.
I was once caught having stolen some apples along with two or three other inmates, which I did.
And I think I was beaten, but it wasn't-- it wasn't life-threatening.
Yes, I was always able to get extra food.
Well, for instance, the apples I mentioned, and I was able to sometimes get potatoes that were kept in a certain place.
And I would get-- they were raw potatoes.
Especially in this last camp that I was in after Kaufering, we would take potatoes and roast them, and supplement our food-- roast them in little heaps of fire that we made.
Well, this-- I must say that this happened after the Germans had left.
I was able to procure extra food here and there.
I don't remember exactly.
But I know I ended up very often with more ration.
I know I didn't steal it from others.
But I was able to procure it, or people would give it to me.
Or maybe a German guard took a liking to me, knowing I was speaking German, and would maybe give it to me.
There were such people.
Yes, it helped me.
Well, what happened, especially towards the end of the war, most of the inmates, most of the interned people, were from Eastern Europe.
They were either from Hungary, or from Poland, or from Russia, or from East Germany-- the eastern part of Germany.
And they did not speak German.
They either spoke Yiddish, or they spoke Polish, or they spoke Russian or Hungarian.
And I spoke fluent German, so that guards hearing-- there were very few German Jews in the camps during the whole time that I was there.
They were definitely in the minority.
And so when German guards or German staff heard German spoken by inmates, they, they somehow took note.
And that was an advantage to me, which sometimes worked in my favor.
Well, in that I was able to communicate.
I was able to understand what the German guards were saying.
I was able to act as an interpreter at times, if the situation required it.
And I was trying to just kind of act as a-- as a little bit of an intermediate to make matters more tolerable.
Yeah.
When I say they did that, which they did, it was-- it didn't happen very often.
It didn't happen very often, but it contributed to my survival.
